section.title: War Against Benjamin
section.sectionRationale: The dismembered summons of chapter 19 gathers all Israel to Mizpah, four hundred thousand strong, to answer the atrocity at Gibeah. When Benjamin refuses to hand over the guilty men and instead takes up arms to defend them, the confrontation becomes a horrifying civil war. Israel inquires of the LORD and yet is twice cut down before Benjamin; only on the third day, by ambush and by the LORD's own hand, is Benjamin defeated and all but annihilated. It is one movement: righteous outrage curdling into near fratricide, and a tribe reduced to six hundred survivors.
section.thesis: Israel is right to be horrified and right to seek justice, yet in a land with no king and no clear leader the pursuit of justice slides into a catastrophic civil war that nearly destroys a brother tribe, so that even the punishing of evil is drenched in loss and folly, and the reader aches for a King who will judge in wisdom and mercy.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the assembly at Mizpah and Benjamin's refusal, verses 1 to 17; the two costly defeats of Israel despite inquiring of God, verses 18 to 28; the ambush and the LORD's defeat of Benjamin, verses 29 to 41; and the near-annihilation of the tribe, verses 42 to 48.

units.label: The assembly at Mizpah and Benjamin's refusal

verses.text: Then all the Israelites from Dan to Beersheba and from the land of Gilead came out, and the {{edah|congregation}} assembled as one man before the LORD at Mizpah.
The leaders of all the people and all the tribes of Israel presented themselves in the assembly of God’s people: 400,000 men on foot, armed with swords.
(Meanwhile the Benjamites heard that the Israelites had gone up to Mizpah.) And the Israelites asked, “Tell us, how did this wicked thing happen?”
So the Levite, the husband of the murdered woman, answered: “I and my concubine came to Gibeah in Benjamin to spend the night.
And during the night, the men of Gibeah rose up against me and surrounded the house. They intended to kill me, but they abused my concubine, and she died.
Then I took my concubine, cut her into pieces, and sent her throughout the land of Israel’s inheritance, because they had committed a lewd and {{nebalah|disgraceful act}} in Israel.
Behold, all you Israelites, give your advice and verdict here and now.”
Then all the people stood as one man and said, “Not one of us will return to his tent or to his house.
Now this is what we will do to Gibeah: We will go against it as the lot dictates.

We will take ten men out of every hundred from all the tribes of Israel, and a hundred out of every thousand, and a thousand out of every ten thousand, to supply provisions for the army when they go to Gibeah in Benjamin to punish them for the atrocity they have committed in Israel.”
So all the men of Israel gathered as one man, united against the city.
And the tribes of Israel sent men throughout the tribe of Benjamin, saying, “What is this wickedness that has occurred among you?
Hand over the wicked men of Gibeah so we can put them to death and purge Israel of this evil.” But the Benjamites refused to heed the voice of their fellow Israelites.
And from their cities they came together at Gibeah to go out and fight against the Israelites.
On that day the Benjamites mobilized 26,000 swordsmen from their cities, in addition to the 700 select men of Gibeah.
Among all these soldiers there were 700 select left-handers, each of whom could sling a stone at a hair without missing.
The Israelites, apart from Benjamin, mobilized 400,000 swordsmen, each one an experienced warrior.

units.label: Two costly defeats despite inquiring of God

verses.text: The Israelites set out, went up to Bethel, and {{shaal|inquired}} of God, “Who of us shall go up first to fight against the Benjamites?” “Judah will be first,” the LORD replied.
The next morning the Israelites set out and camped near Gibeah.
And the men of Israel went out to fight against Benjamin and took up their battle positions at Gibeah.
And the Benjamites came out of Gibeah and cut down 22,000 Israelites on the battlefield that day.
But the Israelite army took courage and again took their battle positions in the same place where they had arrayed themselves on the first day.
They went up and wept before the LORD until evening, inquiring of Him, “Should we again draw near for battle against our brothers the Benjamites?” And the LORD answered, “Go up against them.”
On the second day the Israelites advanced against the Benjamites.
That same day the Benjamites came out against them from Gibeah and cut down another 18,000 Israelites, all of them armed with swords.
Then the Israelites, all the people, went up to Bethel, where they sat weeping before the LORD. That day they fasted until evening and presented burnt offerings and peace offerings to the LORD.
And the Israelites inquired of the LORD. (In those days the ark of the covenant of God was there,

and Phinehas son of Eleazar, the son of Aaron, served before it.) The Israelites asked, “Should we again go out to battle against our brothers the Benjamites, or should we stop?” The LORD answered, “Fight, for tomorrow I will deliver them into your hand.”

units.label: The ambush and the LORD's defeat of Benjamin

verses.text: So Israel set up an ambush around Gibeah.
On the third day the Israelites went up against the Benjamites and arrayed themselves against Gibeah as they had done before.

The Benjamites came out against them and were drawn away from the city. They began to attack the people as before, killing about thirty men of Israel in the fields and on the roads, one of which led up to Bethel and the other to Gibeah.
“We are defeating them as before,” said the Benjamites. But the Israelites said, “Let us retreat and draw them away from the city onto the roads.”
So all the men of Israel got up from their places and arrayed themselves at Baal-tamar, and the Israelites in ambush charged from their positions west of Gibeah.
Then 10,000 select men from all Israel made a frontal assault against Gibeah, and the battle was fierce. But the Benjamites did not realize that disaster was upon them.
The LORD defeated Benjamin in the presence of Israel, and on that day the Israelites slaughtered 25,100 Benjamites, all armed with swords.
Then the Benjamites realized they had been defeated. Now the men of Israel had retreated before Benjamin because they were relying on the ambush they had set against Gibeah.
The men in ambush rushed suddenly against Gibeah; they advanced and put the whole city to the sword.
The men of Israel had arranged a signal with the men in ambush: When they sent up a great cloud of smoke from the city,
the men of Israel would turn in the battle. When the Benjamites had begun to strike them down, killing about thirty men of Israel, they said, “They are defeated before us as in the first battle.”
But when the column of smoke began to go up from the city, the Benjamites looked behind them and saw the whole city going up in smoke.
Then the men of Israel turned back on them, and the men of Benjamin were terrified when they realized that disaster had come upon them.
So they fled before the men of Israel toward the wilderness, but the battle overtook them, and the men coming out of the cities struck them down there.
They surrounded the Benjamites, pursued them, and easily overtook them in the vicinity of Gibeah on the east.
And 18,000 Benjamites fell, all men of valor.
Then the Benjamites turned and fled toward the wilderness to the rock of Rimmon, and Israel cut down 5,000 men on the roads. And they overtook them at Gidom and struck down 2,000 more.
That day 25,000 Benjamite swordsmen fell, all men of valor.
But 600 men turned and fled into the wilderness to the rock of Rimmon, where they stayed four months.

And the men of Israel turned back against the other Benjamites and put to the sword all the cities, including the animals and everything else they found. And they burned down all the cities in their path.

edah.gloss: Congregation, assembly, the whole community of Israel gathered as one body. The word marks the tribes acting together as one man before the LORD at Mizpah. It carries covenant weight: this is Israel assembled for a solemn, corporate reckoning, which makes the ensuing near-destruction of one of its own members all the more tragic.

nebalah.gloss: Outrage, disgraceful folly, wanton covenant-shattering wickedness. The Levite names the crime at Gibeah a nebalah, the same word the old man used in chapter 19. Israel is right to call the deed an atrocity; the tragedy is that the just naming of evil gives way to a war that itself becomes a catastrophe.

shaal.gloss: To ask, inquire, seek a word from God. Israel repeatedly inquires of the LORD at Bethel before the ark, verses 18, 23, 27 to 28. They genuinely seek God's direction, yet are twice defeated before the promised victory comes; the word underlines that even sincere seeking unfolds under God's inscrutable purposes, and that success is finally His gift, not their entitlement.

basic.context: The atrocity at Gibeah brings all Israel together at Mizpah, an enormous army united as one man to demand justice. The Levite gives his account, and the assembly resolves to punish Gibeah. When they ask Benjamin to hand over the guilty men, Benjamin refuses and instead musters to defend them, and the confrontation becomes civil war. Israel inquires of the LORD, who tells them to go up, yet they are cut down, twenty-two thousand the first day and eighteen thousand the second. Weeping, fasting, and offering sacrifices, they inquire again, and God promises victory. By a planned ambush, and above all by the LORD's own defeat of Benjamin, the tribe is routed and all but wiped out; only six hundred men escape to the rock of Rimmon, and Benjamin's towns are burned. The chapter asks a hard question: what happens to justice when there is no king to administer it wisely?
basic.hermeneutics: This is sober historical narrative, not a template for war or for church discipline. Israel's horror at the crime is right, and their desire to purge evil has warrant in the law. Yet the account is heavy with tragedy: brothers slaughtering brothers, two devastating defeats even after inquiring of God, and a punishment that spirals into near-genocide and the burning of whole cities. We should neither whitewash Israel's excesses nor doubt that the original crime deserved judgment; the narrator lets both the justice and the horror stand, under the frame that there was no king.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel gathers as one edah, one congregation, acting as a single body before the LORD. The crime is named a nebalah, an outrage, the right verdict on real evil. Three times Israel shaal, inquires of the LORD, and yet is twice defeated before victory comes, a reminder that seeking God is not a lever to force our outcomes but a trust in His purposes, and that the victory, when it comes, is expressly the LORD's doing.
basic.typology: The costly, confused pursuit of justice here points by contrast to the perfect justice of Christ, who judges rightly and without excess, in whom mercy and truth meet. The two defeats before victory show that even the punishing of evil, in human hands and in a fallen world, is drenched in loss; only the King who is to come can execute judgment in wisdom and restraint.
basic.christ: Israel is right to seek justice, yet without a righteous king they cannot administer it without terrible collateral ruin, brothers destroyed, a tribe nearly erased. This drives us to Christ, the King who alone judges the world in righteousness, Acts 17:31, who does not break the bruised reed, and who satisfies justice not by annihilating His enemies but by bearing judgment Himself so that mercy can triumph. Where Israel's sword nearly ended a tribe, Christ's cross opens the way for the guilty to be spared.
basic.god: God is sovereign over this whole terrible chapter. He answers when Israel inquires, He permits two crushing defeats that humble a self-confident army, and in the end it is expressly the LORD who defeats Benjamin. He is holy and will not let the outrage go unpunished, yet His ways are not simple or swift, and He works even through Israel's grief and folly. The chapter warns us against treating God as a tool for our causes, and calls us to trust a justice higher and wiser than our own.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 13 gives the command to purge evil from among the people, the warrant Israel invokes. Judges 19 records the crime that provoked the war. Genesis 49 foretells Benjamin as a ravenous wolf, sadly fulfilled here. And Acts 17 declares that God will judge the world in righteousness through the risen Christ.
basic.oneStory: The war against Benjamin shows a people right to hate evil but unable, with no king, to answer it without catastrophe. The near-loss of a whole tribe is a wound in the body of Israel that the closing chapter will scramble to heal. It leaves us longing for the King who judges justly and mercifully, fulfilled in Christ, who bears the judgment we deserve so that even His enemies may be reconciled.

context.literary: The second scene of the epilogue, flowing directly from the summons of chapter 19. It is told with grim, repetitive structure: three days of battle, two defeats, one victory; three inquiries of God; escalating weeping and sacrifice. The refrain our brothers the Benjamites keeps the tragedy of fratricide before us. The mustering of all Israel as one man contrasts sharply with the fragmentation of the tribes earlier in the book, but here their unity is bent toward the destruction of one of their own.
context.historical: Mizpah and Bethel were covenant gathering places, and the ark and the priest Phinehas locate the events early in the period of the judges, soon after Joshua's generation. To inquire of the LORD before the ark was the appointed way to seek divine guidance for war. Benjamin's refusal to surrender the guilty men of Gibeah made the whole tribe complicit; the resulting war followed the brutal logic of ancient conflict, including the destruction of towns. The seven hundred left-handed slingers reflect a known Benjamite reputation for skilled fighters.
context.canonical: This civil war stands between the atrocity of chapter 19 and the desperate schemes of chapter 21, forming the book's dark climax. It fulfils, tragically, Jacob's word that Benjamin would be a ravenous wolf, Genesis 49:27, and it foreshadows the later grief of Israel divided against itself. In the sweep of Scripture it exposes the limits of human justice apart from a righteous king and prepares, by contrast, for the King who will judge the nations rightly, and for the cross where justice and mercy are reconciled.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Israel's horror at the crime was right and yet, with no king to lead in wisdom, the pursuit of justice collapsed into a fratricidal war that nearly destroyed a brother tribe, so that the reader feels both the necessity of judging evil and the tragedy of doing so without a righteous king.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific, tragic war; it does not prescribe holy war, vengeance, or the destruction of whole communities for believers. What endures is the seriousness of sin that must be answered, the danger of self-confidence even when we seek God, God's sovereignty over outcomes, and the longing for a King who judges in perfect wisdom and mercy.

debates.question: Was Israel's war against Benjamin just?

views.label: Just in cause but excessive in execution
views.case: The crime was a genuine nebalah, and the law commanded purging such evil; Benjamin's refusal to give up the guilty made confrontation right. Yet the war spiraled into near-genocide, the burning of every town and the killing of animals and non-combatants, going far beyond justice into vengeance.

views.label: Compromised from the start
views.case: Israel could weep and unite against Benjamin but had shown no such zeal against the Levite's own conduct or the wider corruption of the age; the very army was formed under the frame there was no king, and its confused, self-confident violence reflects a nation doing what seemed right in its own eyes.

debates.bearing: Either reading warns that even righteous indignation, in the hands of sinners with no righteous ruler, can become a source of fresh evil, and drives us to the King who judges without partiality and without excess.

debates.question: Why did Israel lose twice after inquiring of the LORD and being told to go up?

views.label: To humble their self-confidence
views.case: The first inquiry only asks who should go up first, assuming victory; the crushing defeats bring Israel from strategy to weeping, fasting, and sacrifice, teaching them that success is God's gift, not their entitlement, before He grants the promised victory.

views.label: Because God's purposes are inscrutable and just
views.case: God had His own reckoning with Israel's wider sin, not only Benjamin's; He permitted the losses within His holy and hidden purposes, so that the final victory is unmistakably His doing, verse 35, and not the achievement of a righteous army.

debates.bearing: Both guard us from treating inquiry of God as a lever to force outcomes; we seek Him in humility, trust His wisdom when He delays, and credit any victory to Him alone.

study.originalLanguages: The tribes come together as one edah, one congregation, gathered before the LORD, and the word carries covenant solemnity: this is not a mob but the assembled people of God, which makes the near-destruction of one of their own the more grievous. The Levite names the crime a nebalah, an outrage, and Israel rightly recoils from real evil. Yet the chapter's most telling verb is shaal, to inquire: three times Israel seeks a word from the LORD at Bethel, before the ark and the priest Phinehas. They are not acting without reference to God; they genuinely ask. And still they are cut down twice before the promised victory comes, and when it comes the text says plainly that the LORD defeated Benjamin, verse 35. The vocabulary holds together three truths: the crime was truly outrageous, the seeking of God was real, and the victory was God's gift and not Israel's entitlement. Even sincere devotion unfolds under God's inscrutable and holy purposes, and success is His to give.

typology.type: Israel's costly, excessive pursuit of justice
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the King who judges the world in perfect righteousness and restraint, Acts 17:31 and Isaiah 11:3-4.

typology.type: Two defeats before the LORD grants victory
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of humbling before deliverance, fulfilled supremely as Christ is brought low before He is exalted to save, Philippians 2:8-9.

typology.type: A brother tribe nearly destroyed by the sword
typology.fulfillment: Christ who reconciles enemies and brothers by absorbing judgment in His own body rather than destroying the guilty, Ephesians 2:14-16.

study.christ: Israel's horror at the outrage is right, and its desire to purge evil rests on God's law; yet without a righteous king the nation cannot answer evil without brother slaughtering brother and a whole tribe brought to the edge of extinction. The chapter thus preaches its own lack: it cries out for the King who judges the world in righteousness, Acts 17:31, who will not break a bruised reed or quench a smoldering wick, Isaiah 42:3, and who satisfies justice not by annihilating His enemies but by bearing their judgment Himself. Where Israel's sword nearly ended Benjamin, Christ's cross opens the way for the guilty to be spared and even for enemies to be made brothers. And where Israel is twice humbled before victory, Christ is humbled to death before He is raised to reign, winning by losing the salvation His people could never seize by their own strength.
study.god: God presides over this whole dark chapter. When Israel inquires, He answers; when they presume, He humbles them with defeat; and when the hour comes, it is expressly the LORD who defeats Benjamin, not Israel's numbers or skill. He is holy, and He will not let the outrage stand unpunished, yet His ways are neither simple nor swift, and He works His just purposes through Israel's grief, folly, and loss alike. The chapter refuses to let us treat God as a weapon for our causes; He is the Judge whose wisdom is higher than ours, who cannot be manipulated by our inquiries, and who calls us to trust a justice deeper and purer than any we could execute. That justice will finally be revealed and satisfied in Christ.

crossReferences.note: The command to purge the evil from among you, the warrant Israel invokes against Gibeah and Benjamin.
The atrocity at Gibeah and the dismembered summons that gathered Israel for this war.
Benjamin is a ravenous wolf; his fierce, self-destructive violence is tragically displayed here.
God has set a day when He will judge the world in righteousness through the risen Christ, the perfect Judge Israel lacked.

study.oneStory: The war against Benjamin shows a people right to hate evil but unable, with no king, to answer it without catastrophe: brothers destroyed, sacred inquiries followed by crushing defeat, and a tribe nearly erased from Israel. It is the dark heart of the book's argument that self-rule devours even the covenant family. The wound left here will drive the desperate schemes of the final chapter, and the whole tragedy leans toward the righteous King who judges justly and mercifully, Christ, who bears the judgment His people deserve so that even enemies may be reconciled as brothers.

quiz.q: Why did all Israel assemble at Mizpah?

options.text: To answer the atrocity at Gibeah and seek justice for the crime committed in Israel.
options.note: The dismembered summons of chapter 19 brought the tribes together as one to reckon with the evil.

options.text: To crown a new king over the nation.
options.note: There was no king; the assembly was about justice, though the whole book longs for a king.

options.text: To celebrate a yearly feast to the LORD.
options.note: A feast appears in chapter 21, not here; this gathering was for judgment.

options.text: To divide the land among the tribes.
options.note: The land was already allotted; the assembly gathered over the crime at Gibeah.

quiz.q: How did Benjamin respond to Israel's demand to hand over the guilty men of Gibeah?

options.text: They refused and mustered to defend the guilty, turning the confrontation into civil war.
options.note: Verses 13 to 14 show Benjamin taking up arms rather than purging the evil among them.

options.text: They handed the men over to be put to death.
options.note: The opposite: had they done so, war might have been avoided; they refused.

options.text: They asked for time to consider the matter.
options.note: They did not deliberate; they refused and gathered to fight.

options.text: They fled the land entirely.
options.note: They stood and fought at Gibeah rather than fleeing.

quiz.q: What happened when Israel first went up against Benjamin after inquiring of God?

options.text: Israel was cut down, losing 22,000 the first day and 18,000 the second, before the LORD finally gave victory.
options.note: Verses 21 and 25 record two crushing defeats before the third-day victory.

options.text: Israel won easily on the first day.
options.note: Victory came only on the third day; the first two days were costly defeats.

options.text: Both armies made peace without fighting.
options.note: There was no peace; the war was terrible and bloody.

options.text: God refused to answer them at all.
options.note: God did answer their inquiries, yet allowed two defeats before granting the win.

quiz.q: Who is finally credited with the defeat of Benjamin?

options.text: The LORD, who defeated Benjamin in the presence of Israel.
options.note: Verse 35 says plainly that the LORD defeated Benjamin; the victory was His gift.

options.text: The seven hundred left-handed slingers of Benjamin.
options.note: The slingers were on Benjamin's side; the victory belonged to Israel and, ultimately, to the LORD.

options.text: The cleverness of the ambush alone.
options.note: The ambush was the means, but the text credits the LORD Himself for the defeat.

options.text: The priest Phinehas leading the charge.
options.note: Phinehas ministered before the ark; the text credits the LORD, not his generalship.

quiz.q: How does this tragic war point us toward Christ?

options.text: It exposes the limits of human justice without a righteous king and makes us long for Christ, who judges rightly and bears judgment Himself to spare the guilty.
options.note: The catastrophe cries out for the King who judges in wisdom and mercy, fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: It teaches that destroying our enemies is the path to peace.
options.note: The war nearly destroyed a brother tribe; Christ reconciles enemies rather than annihilating them.

options.text: It shows that God always gives quick, easy victories to the righteous.
options.note: Israel was twice defeated; the chapter humbles such assumptions.

options.text: It has no bearing on Christ or the gospel.
options.note: The longing for a righteous Judge and King runs straight to Christ.

questions: Israel was right to be horrified by the crime, yet their response spiraled into terrible excess. How can righteous anger at evil, in your own life, curdle into something destructive, and what guards against it?
Israel inquired of the LORD and still met defeat before victory came. How do you respond when you seek God sincerely and things go hard before they go well?
The refrain our brothers the Benjamites keeps the tragedy of fratricide before us. Where are you tempted to treat fellow believers as enemies to be destroyed rather than brothers to be won?
The text says plainly that the LORD defeated Benjamin. How does crediting every real victory to God, rather than to our strategy or strength, reshape the way you fight your battles?

This chapter shows that human justice without a righteous king ends in catastrophe. How does it move you to trust Christ, the perfect Judge, who bears the judgment we deserve so that even enemies can be reconciled?

translationNotes.note: The BSB reads Gibeah, following one Hebrew manuscript; a footnote records that most Hebrew manuscripts read Geba, a variant name for the same town. The sense is unchanged: provisions for the army going against Gibeah.
Seven hundred select left-handers reflects a known Benjamite skill; the name Benjamin means son of the right hand, so the note that they were left-handed carries a quiet irony.

The place from which the ambush charged is given by the BSB as west of Gibeah, following some Septuagint manuscripts and the Vulgate; the Hebrew is uncertain and reads Maareh-geba or the open space of Geba.

The LORD defeated Benjamin makes explicit that the decisive blow was God's, even though Israel set the ambush and wielded the sword; the numbers here and in verse 46 are given in round and precise forms that Scripture holds together.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 20:1-48, all 48 verses. Prose narrative. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. Handled soberly as description not prescription. Sermon set to none; no message from a trusted cluster was verified this session.
